stimulus:
You have learned the instructions for the task! Here is a demo trial.

Like what you will do in the main experiment,

you will watch a movie clip and at the same time mark off the activity in the movie into the smallest meaningful units that seem natural and meaningful to you.

Remember, tap the SPACE BAR to mark off a boundary between two smallest meaningful units of activity.

A green bar will appear at the bottom of the screen when you press the button.

Remember that there is no right or wrong answer.

We just want to know which parts of the activity seem natural and meaningful to you.

Now the main experiment starts.

For each clip, while watching, remember to press the SPACE BAR whenever one smallest meaningful unit of activity ends and another begins.

stimulus:
What are we trying to learn in this research?

In this study, we would like to understand factors that affect people's perception of boundaries, or timepoints of transitions, between events. We hypothesize that unpredictable actions will lead to the perception of more event boundaries.

Why is this important to scientists or the general public?

This can help us understand how we process surprising actions, as well as how temporal information is perceived and remembered in complex and dynamic experiences.

Where can I learn more?

Zacks, J. M. (2020). Event perception and memory. Annual Review of Psychology, 71(1), 165–191.

Ben-Yakov, A., Smith, V., & Henson, R. (2022). The limited reach of surprise: Evidence against effects of surprise on memory for preceding elements of an event. Psychonomic Bulletin & Review, 29(3), 1053–1064.

Kumar, M., Goldstein, A., Michelmann, S., Zacks, J. M., Hasson, U., & Norman, K. A. (2023). Bayesian surprise predicts human event segmentation in story listening. Cognitive Science, 47(10), e13343.

What if I have questions later?

Please contact Oliver Gu (g.yukai@wustl.edu) or Yining Ding (d.yining@wustl.edu) from the Dynamic Cognition Lab, PI: Jeffrey M. Zacks.
